model_narrative: We schedule a set of aircraft that are approaching an airport with several runways. For each aircraft we decide its landing time, which runway it is assigned to, and the order in which aircraft sharing a runway land relative to one another. Each aircraft has a scheduled target landing time, and landing later than that target incurs a per-unit-time penalty that varies by aircraft. The objective is to minimize the total delay penalty summed over all aircraft.

sets.name: N
sets.doc: the set of aircraft currently in approach, indexed by integers; ordering of the indices reflects no priority

sets.name: K
sets.doc: the set of available runways, indexed by integers

params.name: c_plus
params.doc: the penalty charged per unit of time that an aircraft lands after its scheduled time, in cost per time unit

params.name: S
params.doc: the minimum required separation time between two aircraft that use the same runway in sequence; only defined for ordered pairs where the first index is strictly smaller than the second, with value 5 time units for every such pair

params.name: E
params.doc: the earliest time at which each aircraft is allowed to land, in time units; equal to 5 for every aircraft

params.name: L
params.doc: the latest time by which each aircraft is allowed to land, in time units; equal to 1000 for every aircraft

params.name: T
params.doc: the originally scheduled (target) landing time of each aircraft, in time units; equal to 5 for every aircraft

params.name: M
params.doc: a single large constant used to deactivate the separation inequalities when the relevant ordering or runway-assignment conditions do not hold; a scalar with value 20

vars.name: w
vars.doc: ordering indicator over a pair of aircraft; equals 1 if the first aircraft lands before the second and 0 otherwise

vars.name: x
vars.doc: runway assignment indicator; equals 1 if the aircraft is allocated to the runway and 0 otherwise

vars.name: t
vars.doc: the landing time of each aircraft, in time units

constraints.name: arrival_time_constr
constraints.intent: Every aircraft must land no earlier than its originally scheduled target time, so each aircraft either lands on time or later than planned but never ahead of schedule.

constraints.name: upper_time_window_constr
constraints.intent: Each aircraft must land by its latest permitted time. For every aircraft the chosen landing time cannot exceed the latest time it is allowed to land.

constraints.name: lower_time_window_constr
constraints.intent: Each aircraft must land no earlier than its earliest permitted time. For every aircraft the chosen landing time must be at least the earliest time it is allowed to land.

constraints.name: assignment_constr
constraints.intent: Every aircraft must be assigned to exactly one runway. For each aircraft, the assignment across all runways must add up to a single runway.

constraints.name: sequencing_constr1
constraints.intent: When one aircraft is set to land before another and both are using the same runway, the later one cannot land until enough separation time has passed after the earlier one. For each pair of aircraft and each runway, whenever the first aircraft is ordered before the second and both are assigned to that runway, the first aircraft's landing time plus the required separation must not exceed the second aircraft's landing time. This requirement is enforced only under that ordering and that shared runway, and is otherwise relaxed so it does not bind. For pairs that are not in this forward order the requirement reduces to the plain rule that an aircraft lands no earlier than its scheduled target time.

constraints.name: sequencing_constr2
constraints.intent: This is the companion separation requirement for the opposite landing order. When the second aircraft of a pair is set to land before the first and both share the same runway, the first one cannot land until enough separation time has passed after the second. For each pair of aircraft and each runway, whenever the ordering puts the second aircraft first and both are assigned to that runway, the second aircraft's landing time plus the required separation must not exceed the first aircraft's landing time. The requirement is enforced only under that ordering and that shared runway, and is otherwise relaxed so it does not bind. For pairs that are not in this configuration the requirement reduces to the plain rule that an aircraft lands no earlier than its scheduled target time.

query: Runway 1's lighting is being serviced tomorrow morning, so dispatch wants to keep it to a single landing for the day. With at most one aircraft allowed on runway 1, what does the optimizer return for total delay cost?
expected_pyomo: model.runway1_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i, 1] for i in model.N) <= 1)

query: We're weighing a tower policy that sends the two most expensive flights, aircraft 4 and 5, both to runway 2 to keep them off the busy approach. If both 4 and 5 are pinned to runway 2, what total cost does the plan come to?

query: We're trialing a new arrival-metering procedure that spreads the inbound stream out instead of bunching everyone onto the threshold. Under that procedure the combined time-on-approach across all five aircraft has to add up to at least 52 time units. With that floor on the total of the landing times, where does the delay bill land?
expected_pyomo: model.min_total_time = Constraint(expr=sum(model.t[i] for i in model.N) >= 52)

query: Engineering wants the two heaviest aircraft, 4 and 5, kept together on a single strip for wake-turbulence handling. If aircraft 4 and 5 must share runway 1, what's the resulting total delay cost?

query: It feels wasteful to reorder the queue — wouldn't it be cleaner to just land them strictly in their arrival order, aircraft 1, then 2, then 3, and so on? The plan reshuffles them and I don't see why; what's the model trading off?
expected_pyomo: model.fifo_order = Constraint(model.N, model.N, rule=lambda m, i, j: (m.t[i] <= m.t[j]) if i < j else Constraint.Skip)

query: Aircraft 1 is low on fuel, so my instinct says it should touch down first, ahead of everyone else. The optimizer doesn't put it first and I want to understand the cost of overriding that — what does forcing aircraft 1 to land no later than all the others do to the plan?
expected_pyomo: model.a1_first = Constraint(model.N, rule=lambda m, j: (m.t[1] <= m.t[j]) if j != 1 else Constraint.Skip)

query: Aircraft 5 keeps getting slotted in at the very front, right on top of aircraft 1, which makes me nervous — it's the heaviest in the stream and I'd expect it to follow aircraft 1 in by a comfortable margin rather than crowd it. Wouldn't it be safer to require aircraft 5 to touch down at least three time units after aircraft 1 does? The optimizer won't space them out like that on its own — what's keeping it from making that move?

vars.doc: runway assignment indicator; equals 1 if the aircraft is allocated to the runway and 0 otherwise

vars.name: t
vars.doc: the landing time of each aircraft, in time units

constraints.name: arrival_time_constr
constraints.intent: Every aircraft must land no earlier than its originally scheduled target time, so each aircraft either lands on time or later than planned but never ahead of schedule.

constraints.name: upper_time_window_constr
constraints.intent: Each aircraft must land by its latest permitted time. For every aircraft the chosen landing time cannot exceed the latest time it is allowed to land.

constraints.name: lower_time_window_constr
constraints.intent: Each aircraft must land no earlier than its earliest permitted time. For every aircraft the chosen landing time must be at least the earliest time it is allowed to land.

constraints.name: assignment_constr
constraints.intent: Every aircraft must be assigned to exactly one runway. For each aircraft, the assignment across all runways must add up to a single runway.

constraints.name: sequencing_constr1
constraints.intent: When one aircraft is set to land before another and both are using the same runway, the later one cannot land until enough separation time has passed after the earlier one. For each pair of aircraft and each runway, whenever the first aircraft is ordered before the second and both are assigned to that runway, the first aircraft's landing time plus the required separation must not exceed the second aircraft's landing time. This requirement is enforced only under that ordering and that shared runway, and is otherwise relaxed so it does not bind. For pairs that are not in this forward order the requirement reduces to the plain rule that an aircraft lands no earlier than its scheduled target time.

constraints.name: sequencing_constr2
constraints.intent: This is the companion separation requirement for the opposite landing order. When the second aircraft of a pair is set to land before the first and both share the same runway, the first one cannot land until enough separation time has passed after the second. For each pair of aircraft and each runway, whenever the ordering puts the second aircraft first and both are assigned to that runway, the second aircraft's landing time plus the required separation must not exceed the first aircraft's landing time. The requirement is enforced only under that ordering and that shared runway, and is otherwise relaxed so it does not bind. For pairs that are not in this configuration the requirement reduces to the plain rule that an aircraft lands no earlier than its scheduled target time.

query: Runway 1 sits right by the terminal and I'd rather keep the three later aircraft, 3, 4 and 5, on the quieter runway 2. The plan splits them across both strips instead — what's the reason it won't just cluster those three on runway 2?
